does: This paper proposes Federated Memorization Pruning (FedMemPrune), a pruning-based approach to federated unlearning that distinguishes between memorized information specific to forgotten data and overlapping knowledge shared with remaining data. The authors introduce Grouped Memorization Evaluation, an example-level metric that quantifies unique memorization versus common patterns across datasets. FedMemPrune resets parameters responsible for storing unique memorized information while preserving those supporting shared patterns. Experimental results demonstrate that FedMemPrune achieves unlearning performance approaching full retraining baselines while more effectively eliminating memorization compared to existing federated unlearning methods, addressing fairness concerns between clients in distributed settings.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of federated learning and machine unlearning, extending prior efforts in privacy-preserving FL that handle deletion requests under regulations like GDPR. It responds to limitations in existing federated unlearning approaches that fail to account for information overlap between data to be forgotten and retained data. The paper connects to broader literature on machine memorization and forgetting, particularly work analyzing what neural networks memorize versus learn as generalizable patterns. It also relates to parameter pruning techniques in FL and privacy-preserving learning more broadly, synthesizing insights from memorization studies with federated optimization to address the overlooked problem of client fairness in distributed unlearning scenarios.
enables: This work opens directions for more principled federated unlearning by establishing memorization as a core lens for evaluating forgetting quality. The example-level metric could enable development of fine-grained unlearning methods that selectively target memorized information across heterogeneous federated datasets. Future work might extend the memorization-based framework to other FL scenarios with non-IID data distributions or explore theoretical guarantees on memorization removal. The approach could facilitate better privacy-utility tradeoffs in regulated federated systems and inspire similar memorization-aware strategies for other privacy-preserving learning settings, including personalized FL where understanding individual client memorization becomes critical.
